text: Welcome to Fragmented, the podcast where we capture what's top of mind for Android developers.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I'm your host, Kaushik.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: In our last episode, we announced a big reboot for Fragmented.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: From now on, I want to tackle topics that Android developers are actively discussing, excited about, or even find frustrating.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So what's on the agenda today?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: One topic that's been both a cornerstone and a thorn for Android developers over the years is dependency injection.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So why is DI interesting now?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: If you start a conversation about DI with any Android developer, what's one word you can almost bet will come up?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Dagger.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But here's the thing.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: While Dagger has been the go-to solution in DI conversations, I think things are changing.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: There's a new king in the dependency injection town, at least in my humble opinion, and it's actually not a single library.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: but a combination of two that work really, really well.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Kotlin Inject and Kotlin Inject Anvil.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: To understand why all of this is significant, I'm going to briefly walk down memory lane and talk about the solutions that have emerged over time.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Then we'll end with the exciting part of this episode where I talk to an expert who actually maintains one of these libraries.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Let's get started.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So back in 2012...
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Square introduced the first version of Dagger or Dagger V1.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: While, you know, even at that time, there were other DI frameworks like Juice, RoboJuice, you know, a bunch of others.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Dagger was designed specifically with Android in mind.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: A core issue in Android is that you cannot instantiate activities or fragments with custom constructors because the framework manages their life cycles.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So traditional constructor injection is just not very straightforward and you cannot manage state effectively across some of these lifecycle changes.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Dagger addressed this and they did this in a clever way through code generation, which was definitely quite novel an idea at the time.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: The generated code would provide a way for framework classes to retrieve dependencies with all the necessary boilerplate that you have to otherwise write.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So what was the problem with Dagger 1?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Why didn't we just keep using it forever?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Performance.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Dagger 1 relied on reflection to generate its boilerplate code.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: If you remember in those days, reflection is a very big no-no in the Android world.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And there was one company in particular that was writing large Android apps and facing these challenges more than anyone.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That company would be Google.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So in 2015, Google released its new version of Dagger, Dagger 2, a complete rewrite aimed specifically at improving performance.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: They moved from reflection to using compile time code generation.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And there was a bunch of other massive performance gains with Dagger 2.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But there was a catch.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: While they made it much more performant, it also introduced a lot of complexity.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Concepts like components were introduced in addition to modules.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And the API honestly just became more intricate and less intuitive.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Setting it up also required like a pretty big, steep learning curve.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Some developers stuck with Dagger 1 because, you know, despite the performance hit, it was simpler.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But when Square officially deprecated Dagger 1 and relinquished stewardship to Google for Dagger, it was kind of time to move on.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But in 2017, again, Google released an update to Dagger 2.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This was Dagger 2.10 or Dagger 2.10.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And in it, there was a specific module or package called Dagger.Android with the hope of reducing boilerplate and making it really easy for Android developers to start using Dagger
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: again.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This came to be known as dagger-android.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So if you see that in blog posts, that's what it refers to.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So did it help?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Not exactly.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Dagger-android also added multiple layers of abstractions, which many found even more confusing.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Instead of simplifying dagger, it sometimes made the dependency graph even harder to follow.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And let's be honest, the documentation up to this point was like notoriously tough.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I, you know, I admire Square, but they started those damn thermosyphon examples.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I had to even learn what a thermosyphon was before understanding the process of coffee making and all the analogies and then finding out how Dagger is to be used.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But anyway, I digress.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: OK, if you're following this timeline somewhat closely, we're in 2017 now.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And something pretty big happened at Google I.O.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: during this year.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So leveraging Kotlin's features, a library emerged aiming to simplify DI even more using Kotlin's DSLs.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And this was coin.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: That's with a K because, you know, this was the dark period where everyone released a Kotlin library and put a K right at the top.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: so why did coin gain popularity honestly there were just two big reasons one it was really easy to use two it was really easy to set up and you know they really leaned heavily on the kotlin and dsl parts of it to make the api so much more pleasant but there was a couple of sticking points even with coin coin used the service locator pattern and this sparked many a debate and flame wars in the community
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And honestly, while this was all helpful in the short term, as apps grew larger, the boilerplate also grew in some ways with this pattern.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: There was also the minor issue of dependencies now being a little more directly coupled to the service locator directly.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: All right, let's move along the timeline.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So seeing some of the challenges that we had with Dagger Android and not even looking at Coin, Google was chugging along in the side and introduced another variant or extension to apply on Dagger.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: called Hilt.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This was as recently as 2020.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So was Hilt better?
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I gotta say, yeah, it is.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And even to this day, it is the official stance and recommendation from Google.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But around the same time, in around 2020, more apps were using Kotlin and apps were becoming larger.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And as a consequence of this, build times were starting to take a hit.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So the whole community shifted towards this philosophy of app modularization.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And I'll tell you this, if you thought integrating Dagger was hard, integrating Dagger into a multi-module app setup was not fun at all.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So the prodigal child of open source Square came back and tackled this problem head-on by introducing...
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: anvil anvil worked alongside dagger and it was another library but it greatly reduced boilerplate by generating factory classes and simplifying the module setup in many ways square open source this at around 2020 and honestly if you don't religiously follow everything that google recommends this is kind of the de facto recommendation from the community today for what it's worth i use this at instacart too for like a lot of the apps
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: In this recap, by the way, I'd be remiss to not mention manual dependency injection as a movement that, you know, some people started to adopt.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: There's just no magic there.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: No code generation, no reflection.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Just keep things simple and wired it up yourself.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I'm personally not a great fan of manual DI, mostly because, you know, apps organically start to become bigger ones and nobody wakes up one day and says, OK, hey, my app today is a big app and everything has become difficult.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: The complexity and boilerplate very similarly starts to creep up if you start to manage things manually.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: But speaking of what I am a fan of, recently a new library has gained traction, Kotlin Inject.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It was started by Eva with the username Avant.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And that name should sound familiar to a lot of us, especially people who used to use...
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: rx java in the java days because there was this library called gradle retro lambda which was a lifesaver so it's the same person who built that library so why is this a big deal firstly kotlin inject simplifies di by not supporting a host of features that you would otherwise find with say dagger 2 as a simple example
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It only has the concept of components, no modules.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This harkens back to the time of Dagger 1, where I actually preferred some of how these things work.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Also, it only supports constructor injection.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: As an Android developer, that might worry you, but I'll add a link to the show notes.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's very much possible to inject into your activities and fragments.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So in not supporting a host of other features, Kotlin Inject actually greatly simplifies the API that you as a developer have to encounter and use.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Second, it's built to work seamlessly with Kotlin Multiplatform, KMP.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This has become such a rallying cry in the community.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And by the way, only Coin and Kotlin Inject support KMP.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: So many people have started to adopt this.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Another development...
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Most recently, as of a month or two ago, a group from Amazon released this library called Kotlin Inject Anvil.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And it's exactly as the name sounds.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: They've effectively taken all the goodness from Anvil and applied it on top of Kotlin Inject and to Chef's Kiss.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: For the first time, I think dependency injection on Android just makes sense.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: The setup is straightforward.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: The concepts are easier to grasp.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: And honestly, the boilerplate is minimal.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: Starting a new project no longer means like, you know, you have to dread your whole deal.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: setup.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: I actually have a simple playground Android app that I'll throw a link in the show notes to where I use Kotlin Inject in a multi-module setup.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: It's simple and honestly I think the DI is very manageable.

speaker: SPEAKER_02
